rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF ERNEST V RHINE
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Re First Amended Will Contest
rulings.Rulings: (Part 1 of 2) Respondent's Motion for Summary Judgement or in the alternative Motion for Summary Adjudication is denied. Respondent did not carry her burden on this Motion to present evidence that either conclusively negates an element of the Petitioner's undue influence cause of action - specially the "undue gain" element - or that presents a complete defense to the cause of action. Nor did she present evidence that the Petitioner does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, needed evidence. Respondent brings her Motion on grounds that the challenged instruments do not give her an "undue benefit." It is undisputed that the decedent and Petitioner were not married at the time the challenged instruments were executed. Further, under the terms of the challenged instruments, even if decedent had married after the instruments' execution, decedent's future spouse would be prevented from inheriting under the omitted spouse and/or pretermitted heir statutes. Respondent, as beneficiary of the Trust, would take as if decent had not remarried. Additionally, the 2000 Trust instrument and the 2004 amended Trust instrument had included charitable gift bequests to third party organizations. (Part 1 of 2)

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF ERNEST V RHINE
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion For Summary Judgment - (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: (Part 2 of 2) Whether this result is "undue" is a factual question. To determine if the beneficiary's profit is "undue" the trier must necessarily decide what profit would be "due" based on the Respondent's and Petitioner's relative connections to decedent as "objects of his bounty." Respondent argues that the testamentary scheme under which she inherits decedent's entire estate, and Petitioner inherits nothing is not "undue," because Respondent was decedent's only child and legal heir when the challenged instruments were executed, and Petitioner does not dispute that she was not a "natural object of decedent's bounty" when the instruments were executed. Although it is undisputed that Petitioner and decedent were not married and Respondent was decedent's only child at the time the instruments were executed, Estate of Sarabia rejects a strict intestacy-based test of what is and is not "undue gain" in a testamentary instrument: Because the law of intestate succession is confined to the decedent's relatives, the test advanced by Winston [challenger to the Will] would invariably work to the advantage of relatives. The test thus threatens to impinge on the testator's right and freedom to devise property to persons or entities with whom the testator has no genetic or legal relationship. 221 Cal. App. 3d 599, 608. This Court will not adopt a version of that rejected test on this Motion. Respondent also presents undisputed evidence of when Petitioner and decedent first met, when they were married, and that Petitioner requested that decedent provide for her in his estate plan during their marriage, but that decedent did not amend his estate plan after executing the challenged 2005 Will and Trust amendment. While undisputed, this evidence does not go so far as to show that the terms of the estate plan are "due" or "undue" to the parties, relative to their relation to decedent and to each other. (Estate of Sarabia (1990) 221 Cal. App. 3d 599, 607). As the moving party, it was Respondent's initial burden to present evidence either conclusively negating, completely defending against, or showing Petitioner's inability to obtain evidence in support of this element of undue influence. She did not, and the burden does not shift to Petitioner to demonstrate the existence of a triable, material issue of fact in her favor on this Motion. The Motion is also brought on the grounds that Petitioner lacks standing to bring these Trust and Will contests, and as a result the Court lacks jurisdiction to hear the petitions. The undisputed evidence presented in the Motion demonstrates that Petitioner is not named as a beneficiary in any of the challenged instruments, or in the decedent's earlier estate planning documents executed in 1998 and 2000, before decedent and Petitioner first met. However, Respondent has not met her burden to present evidence that Petitioner lacks standing to challenge the instruments otherwise. The Court has already ruled in the November 14, 2018 Order overruling Respondent's Demurrer to the Second Amended Petition in the Trust matter that Petition is an "interested person" under Probate Code § 48 in these proceedings. There is no evidence presented to disprove that Petitioner is decedent's surviving spouse. There is no showing on this Motion that the 2000 Trust contains terms negating application of the omitted spouse or pretermitted heir statutes, as the challenged instruments do. The Second Amended Petition in the Trust matter has not been brought under Probate Code § 17200. In the November 14, 2018 order, the Court has declined to construe the petition as being brought under that statute. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) and opposing parties no later than 4:00 p.m. Tuesday, May 21, 2019.
